params.text:
The three main types

There are three main types of stoma, named by where they are made. A colostomy is formed from the large bowel, or colon, and usually passes fairly formed, solid stools. An ileostomy is formed from the end of the small bowel, the ileum, and passes looser, more watery output. A urostomy drains urine, using a short piece of bowel to carry urine from the kidneys out through the stoma after the bladder has been removed or bypassed.

  • Colostomy: from the large bowel — more formed stools.
  • Ileostomy: from the small bowel — looser, watery output.
  • Urostomy: drains urine rather than bowel motions.

Why people have a stoma

People have a stoma for many different reasons, and the decision is always a clinical one. Common reasons include bowel cancer or bladder cancer, inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis, diverticulitis, a blockage or injury to the bowel, or problems controlling the bowel or bladder. A stoma allows waste to pass safely when the usual route cannot be used.

A stoma may be temporary, giving part of the bowel time to rest and heal before it is later reversed, or it may be permanent. Whatever the reason, it is a major change for the person, and how they feel about it matters as much as the practical care. Your role is to support the person and their stoma with respect — never to question the clinical decision behind it.

params.question: Which type of stoma is formed from the large bowel and usually passes fairly formed stools?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A colostomy is made from the large bowel, or colon, and its output is usually more formed.
No — an ileostomy is made from the small bowel and passes looser, watery output.
No — a urostomy drains urine, not bowel motions.
No — a tracheostomy is an opening in the windpipe to help breathing, not a bowel stoma.
